1: Psalm 68 is widely regarded as the most difficult psalm in the Psalter — approximately 20 words appear nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible (hapax legomena), and many verses remain textually uncertain. Its superscription "A Psalm of David. A Song" suggests a combination of genres. The opening "Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered" is a direct quotation of Num 10:35 — the battle cry that was shouted when the ark moved forward through the wilderness. This verbal echo frames the entire psalm as a second Sinai-to-Zion processional: YHWH marches again, as he marched with Israel in the wilderness, now into his sanctuary on Zion.
2: "As smoke is dispersed by the wind... as wax melts before fire" — two destruction similes applied to the enemies of God. The smoke image recurs in Ps 37:20. The melting wax before fire appears in Ps 97:5 (theophany context) and Mic 1:4 (divine judgment). These images echo the Sinai theophany — smoke and fire were the sensory signatures of divine presence (Exod 19:18). The enemies' dissolution before God's presence recalls the Egyptians at the sea (Exod 15:14-16: "Terror and dread fall on them; by the power of your arm they are silent as stone").
3: "Let the righteous be glad and rejoice before God; let them celebrate with great joy" — the contrast with v. 2 is sharp. The same divine presence that destroys the wicked exhilarates the righteous. "Before God (lipnê ʾĕlōhîm)" suggests cultic celebration in the sanctuary — the joy of access to the divine presence. This alternation of threat and celebration mirrors the Sinai experience: the people trembled at the mountain (Exod 20:18-19) while Moses and the elders "ate and drank in the presence of God" (Exod 24:11).
4: "Exalt him who rides upon the heavens" — the divine epithet ʿōlēb bāʿărābôt (rider upon the steppes/clouds/heavens) is a direct claim against Canaanite religion. In Ugaritic myth, Baal is called rkb ʿrpt (rider of the clouds) — the storm-god whose chariot rides through the sky. Israel's polemic: YHWH, not Baal, rides the heavens; YHWH, not Baal, sends rain and controls the storm. By using this title, Ps 68 makes an exclusive territorial claim: the atmospheric powers that Canaan attributed to Baal belong to YHWH alone. See also Ps 18:10; Deut 33:26.
5: "God in his holy dwelling is a father to the fatherless and a defender of widows" — the care for the marginalized is a standard attribute of the righteous king in ANE royal ideology. The preamble to the Code of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BCE) presents Hammurabi as the protector of widows and orphans, the champion of the weak against the strong. Israel claimed this royal social function exclusively for YHWH — the divine king who surpasses all human kings in justice toward the vulnerable. The combination of cosmic sovereignty (v. 4) and social protection (v. 5) is the Psalter's characteristic portrait of YHWH's kingship.
6: "God gives the isolated a home to belong to; he brings prisoners out into freedom; but the rebellious must live in a parched land" — three reversals. (1) The isolated (yĕḥîdîm — solitary, without family) given a household: the deepest social alienation — to lack kin in an honor-shame culture — is addressed by divine provision of belonging. (2) Prisoners brought into freedom (kôšārôt — prosperity, freedom) — the Exodus as paradigm. (3) The rebellious in a parched land — covenantal reversal: the land that should flourish under divine blessing becomes arid for those who reject the covenant.
7: "O God, when you went out before your people, when you marched through the wilderness — Selah" — the poem now moves into the great rehearsal of the wilderness march (vv. 7-10). The language echoes the oldest poetry in the Hebrew Bible: Deborah's Song (Judg 5:4-5) and the Blessing of Moses (Deut 33:2-5). "When you went out before your people (bĕṣēʾtkā liphĕnê ʿammêkā)" frames the Exodus as a military procession, with YHWH as the vanguard commander. Selah invites the congregation to pause and absorb this historical summation before the theophany description continues.
8: "The earth shook and the heavens poured down rain at the presence of God — even Sinai itself trembled — at the presence of the God of Israel" — the Sinai theophany described in terms of cosmic earthquake and storm. Exod 19:16-18 describes thunder, lightning, thick cloud, and smoke as YHWH descended on the mountain. Judg 5:4-5 ("The earth quaked, the heavens poured... the mountains quaked before the LORD") is the closest verbal parallel. The cosmic response to the divine march (earth shaking, rain falling) demonstrates that the entire creation is subject to YHWH — including the weather systems attributed to Baal.
9: "You sent an abundant rain (gešem nĕdābôt), O God; you restored your weary inheritance" — the rain that follows the divine march is both literal (the wilderness becomes fertile at divine command) and covenantal (the land as God's inheritance, naḥălāh). The "weary inheritance" (naḥălātĕkā wĕnilĕʾāh) may refer to Israel exhausted by wilderness wandering, or to the land itself depleted and dry. Nĕdābôt (voluntary, freewill, abundant) suggests that the rain exceeds what was asked — a grace beyond necessity.
10: "Your congregation settled in the land; in your goodness, O God, you provided for the needy (ʾānî)" — the settlement language (yāšĕbû) marks the transition from wilderness wandering to the gift of the land. "In your goodness" (bĕṭûbātĕkā) — divine provision is grounded in God's essential goodness, not human merit. The needy (ʿānî) as the recipients of the land's provision recalls the Levitical jubilee provisions in Lev 25 — the covenant economy designed to protect the vulnerable from permanent dispossession.
11: "The Lord gave the word; great was the army of women who announced the victory (mĕbaśśĕrôt)" — the heralds of divine victory are women in this verse. Mĕbaśśĕrôt (feminine plural of the verb to bring good news, the root of "gospel") echoes Exod 15:20-21, where Miriam and the women sang the victory song after the sea crossing. In Judg 5, Deborah and Jael are central to the victory. The great army of female announcers suggests a celebratory procession of women with tambourines (cf. v. 25; Judg 11:34; 1 Sam 18:6-7), the traditional form for announcing military victory.
12: "The kings of armies fled in haste; she who stayed at home shared in the plunder" — the picture of total victory: the enemy commanders flee so quickly that even those who stayed in camp (women, non-combatants) receive a share of the plunder. This motif appears in Deborah's Song (Judg 5:28-30, where Sisera's mother awaits plunder that never comes) and in the Jericho narrative (Josh 6) where the entire city's wealth goes to YHWH's treasury. The sociological reality: in ANE warfare, non-combatants who stayed behind expected their share of victory goods.
13: "Even if you once lay among the sheepfolds, you shall shine like the wings of a dove, silver-covered" — one of the most obscure verses in the psalm. "Lying among the sheepfolds (šĕpattayim)" may describe a state of inactivity or lowliness before the victory. The dove with silver wings and golden-yellow feathers may be a battle trophy (the ornamented wings of a captured dove?), a reference to Israelite women adorned with plunder, or a symbolic image of transformation from dullness to radiance. The image of transformation through divine victory — from sheep-pen dull to silver-and-gold gleam — is the central movement.
14: "When the Almighty scattered kings throughout the land, snow fell on Zalmon" — Zalmon is likely a mountain near Shechem (Judg 9:48), though exact identification is uncertain. The image of snow whitening the dark mountain after the enemy kings are scattered evokes the sudden reversal of a battle — the battlefield turning white after divine victory. "The Almighty" (šadday) is the archaic divine name associated with the patriarchal narratives (Gen 17:1; 28:3; Exod 6:3) — its use here gives the victory an ancient, primordial quality.
15: "The mountain of God is like the mountain of Bashan — a high, many-peaked mountain" — Bashan (the modern Golan Heights/Hauran region) was famous for its impressive volcanic peaks, particularly Mount Hermon (2,814m). The "mountain of God" in context is Mount Zion — geographically modest (only ~765m) but chosen by divine election. The contrast between the physically imposing Bashan mountains and the divinely chosen Zion mountain is the theological point: YHWH's choice, not topographical majesty, determines the sacred center.
16: "Why do you look with envy (tĕraṣṣĕdûn), O many-peaked mountains? This is the mountain that God chose for his dwelling place; the LORD will dwell there forever" — the mountains of Bashan are personified as envious of Zion. Tĕraṣṣĕdûn (look with envy, lie in wait) suggests hostile surveillance. The theological claim is audacious: the physically impressive mountains of the north are jealous of the small hill in Judah that YHWH chose. Divine election, not natural magnificence, defines sacredness. This theme runs through the prophets (Isa 2:2 — "the mountain of the LORD's temple will be established as the highest mountain").
17: "The chariots of God number in the tens of thousands, thousands upon thousands; the Lord is among them — as at Sinai, in his holy place" — the divine war host. ANE battle accounts counted chariots as the measure of military supremacy (Egypt's 600 chariots in Exod 14:7 was a terrifying force). YHWH's chariots are innumerable. The comparison "as at Sinai" connects the Zion procession to the original Sinai theophany — the same God who appeared at Sinai with his heavenly host now enters his Jerusalem temple with the same overwhelming force. Elijah would later see these chariots (2 Kgs 6:17).
18: "You have ascended to the heights, leading a train of captives; you have received tribute from men — even from the rebellious — so that the LORD God might dwell there" — this verse is cited by Paul in Eph 4:8, but with a significant modification: Paul writes "he gave gifts to men" instead of "received gifts/tribute from men." Scholarly explanations include: (1) Paul following a Targum tradition that already had "gave"; (2) Paul reading the psalm's movement (YHWH ascends and then distributes his captured wealth to his people) as a typological pattern for Christ's ascension and gift of the Spirit; (3) the psalm's own logic — tribute received is then distributed as the victor's largesse. Paul applies the ascension-and-distribution pattern to Christ.
19: "Blessed be the Lord, who day after day carries our burdens for us — the God of our salvation. Selah" — a benediction following the processional account. "Who day after day carries our burdens (yaʿămas-lānû hāʾēl yĕšûʿātēnû)" — the image of YHWH as the burden-bearer who shoulders the weight of his people's daily life, not only in great historical acts. This verse operates at both the cosmic scale (the preceding vv. 7-18) and the daily personal scale — the same God who marched with Israel through the wilderness carries each person's load each day. Selah invites reflection on this transition.
20: "Our God is the God of deliverance; and to the LORD, the Lord, belong the ways of escape from death" — ʾēl lānû ʾēl lĕmôšāʿôt (God to us is a God of deliverances, plural). The plural "deliverances" signals that divine rescue is not a single event but a pattern, a characteristic of YHWH's nature. "The ways of escape from death (tôṣāʾôt lammāwet)" — literally, the exits from death; the paths of escape from the realm of death. The covenantal claim: if YHWH controls even the exits from death, no threat is ultimately beyond his reach to rescue his people from.
21: "But God will crush the head of his enemies, the proud skull of those who persist in their guilt" — crushing the head recalls Gen 3:15 (the enmity between the seed of the woman and the serpent: "he will crush your head"). In ANE victory iconography, the conquered enemy king was depicted prostrate with the victorious king's foot on his head, crushing the skull — a standard posture of total domination. The "proud skull" (qāḏqōḏ) of the one who persists in guilt (ʾašmāt) — continued covenant violation — makes the defeat self-caused: persistent wickedness eventually meets its own crushing.
22: "The Lord declared, 'I will bring them back from Bashan; I will bring my people back from the depths of the sea'" — the reversal promises. Bashan was known as a place of exile and distance (cf. Amos 4:1 — "you cows of Bashan"). The depths of the sea (mimmĕṣulôt yām) echoes the Reed Sea — the cosmic waters of chaos and the historical waters of the Exodus. YHWH will pursue his enemies even to Bashan's heights and the sea's depths — no geographic barrier limits his power to pursue judgment or rescue. The scope of divine sovereignty is total.
23: "So that your foot will be stained in the blood of your enemies, and the tongues of your dogs will lap it up" — the graphic imagery of total military victory. In ANE battle accounts, the complete slaughter of enemies was expressed through the image of blood so abundant that animals lapped it. This hyperbolic language (cf. Judg 5:30; Isa 63:1-6; Rev 14:20) was conventional in ancient military rhetoric — its purpose is to express the totality of divine justice, not to normalize blood-drinking. The dogs feasting on enemy blood recalls 1 Kgs 21:19 (Elijah's word against Ahab) — a marker of shameful death without burial.
24: "People have witnessed your procession, O God — the procession of my God, my King, into the holy place" — the processional scene now depicted as it is seen: a cultic procession into the temple, reenacting (or celebrating) the divine victory march. In Second Temple Judaism, such processionals were liturgical events with real participants — priests, Levites, ark, instruments. The entry of the ark into Jerusalem under David (2 Sam 6) is the historical prototype. The psalm has been moving from the cosmic march (vv. 7-18) to its cultic reenactment (vv. 24-27) — the temple liturgy as the present embodiment of the historical theophany.
25: "The singers lead the procession; behind them come the musicians; among them, young women playing tambourines (ʿălāmôt tôpēpôt)" — the order of the sacred procession: singers first (the choir), then instrumentalists (nōgĕnîm), then young women with tambourines (tōpôt). This matches the victory procession in Exod 15:20-21 (Miriam and women with tambourines) and the processional psalm tradition (Ps 150:4). The three-part musical ensemble was the standard festive processional format for major celebrations. Young women playing tambourines in public was a recognized cultural role for celebrating military and cultic victories.
26: "Bless God in the great assembly (bĕmaqqĕhēlôt); praise the LORD, all you who come from the fountain of Israel (mimmeqôr yiśrāʾēl)" — the invitation to the entire covenant community. "Fountain of Israel" — possibly refers to Jacob (Gen 49:24, "the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel") or to the patriarchal covenant as the source of the nation. The "great assembly" was the technical term for the full gathered congregation of Israel — the formal covenant assembly. To bless God "in the great assembly" is to perform the public, official act of covenant praise.
27: "There is Benjamin, the youngest, leading the way; the princes of Judah in their bright company; the princes of Zebulun, the princes of Naphtali" — four tribes representing the whole nation, but in a theologically loaded order. Benjamin is the smallest and youngest tribe, placed first — divine reversal (the least leads). Judah next — the royal tribe, the Davidic dynasty. Zebulun and Naphtali are northern tribes, the regions later celebrated in Isa 9:1-2 as "the land of darkness" that would see a great light. The ecumenical tribal representation signals national unity in worship before the divine king.
28: "Your God has established your power (ʿuzzĕkā); show your strength, O God, in what you have already begun for us" — the petition draws on what God has already done as the basis for what he is asked to continue. ANE petitions to deities frequently cited the deity's past benefactions as leverage for present requests. In Israelite theology this becomes: your past faithfulness is the grounds for our appeal for continued faithfulness. The divine track record of Exodus and conquest provides the basis for present petition.
29: "Because of your temple in Jerusalem, kings will bring their gifts (yôbîlû mĕlāḵîm šay) to you" — the eschatological vision: foreign kings bringing tribute to YHWH's temple in Jerusalem. This is the Zion pilgrimage theology developed by Isaiah: "Nations will come to your light and kings to the brightness of your rising" (Isa 60:3). The gift-bringing kings appear in Isa 60:5-7, 10-11 and Ps 72:10-11. In Second Temple Judaism, this expectation shaped the interpretation of the Magi's visit in Matt 2:1-12 — Gentile wise men bringing gifts to the divine king in Jerusalem.
30: "Rebuke the beast that lurks in the reeds (ḥayyat qāneh), the herd of bulls among the peoples' young" — the beast in the reeds is Egypt. The Nile's reeds (qāneh) are the natural habitat of the hippopotamus and crocodile — both symbols of Egyptian power in the prophets (Ezek 29:3-5 — Egypt as the great dragon in the Nile; Isa 30:7 — Egypt is Rahab, a chaos monster). Calling Egypt a beast among reeds is a coded political reference. The petition is for YHWH to rebuke Egypt's power over the nations.
31: "Envoys will come from Egypt (minnî miṣrāyim); Ethiopia (kûš) will quickly extend her hands to God" — the eschatological reversal: Egypt, the archetypal oppressor, sends tribute; Ethiopia, the land at the ends of the known world (to Israel's south), stretches out hands in worship. This is the ultimate expansion of the Exodus pattern — not just Israel released from Egypt but Egypt itself turning to YHWH. The universal dimension: the two great Nile-valley powers represent the known world's submission. Isaiah 19:19-25 develops this vision explicitly (Egypt, Assyria, and Israel as three equal peoples of God).
32: "Sing to God, O kingdoms of the earth; sing praises to the Lord. Selah" — the summons broadens from Israel (vv. 24-27) to the tribes (v. 27) to the Gentile nations (v. 31) and now to all kingdoms. The universalization of Sinai worship is the theological trajectory of the whole psalm: what began as a battle cry before the ark becomes a worldwide invitation to praise. Selah marks this climactic expansion — the congregation pauses to absorb that the God who marched from Sinai is the God of all kingdoms.
33: "To him who rides upon the ancient heavens (šĕmê šĕmê-qedem — heavens of the primordial heavens/east)" — a superlative: the highest, oldest heavens, the primordial realm. "Listen, he thunders with his mighty voice" — the storm-theophany tradition: divine speech as thunder (cf. Job 37:5; Ps 29:3-9). In the ANE cosmological framework, the heavens above the visible sky were the realm of the divine council. YHWH's voice penetrating from there to earth is the ultimate demonstration of his sovereign speech-power over creation.
34: "Ascribe power (ʿōz) to God; his splendor is over Israel, and his strength is in the heavens" — the double location of divine strength: "over Israel" (covenant relationship) and "in the heavens" (cosmic sovereignty). The call to "ascribe" (tĕnû) power does not mean God lacks power but that the act of acknowledging his power is itself worship — the community's verbal recognition of reality. This is the theology of Ps 29 (the psalm of the divine voice-in-the-storm), which also ends with a call to ascribe glory and power to YHWH.
35: "God is awe-inspiring (nôrāʾ) from his sanctuary; the God of Israel is the one who gives strength and power to his people. Blessed be God!" — the closing doxology. Nôrāʾ (awesome, fear-inducing) is the standard adjective for divine theophany (Exod 34:10; Deut 7:21; 10:17). The final movement brings together cosmic sovereignty (vv. 4, 33) and covenant care (vv. 5-6, 19) in a single benediction: the fearsome God of the nations is the one who gives strength to his particular people. The psalm closes exactly where it opened — with the divine warrior, but now worshipped rather than petitioned.
